items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Once again I’m plagued
by vague doubts...

Shpak has a tape recorder,
the envoy has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core phrase «терзают смутные сомнения» is well rendered as mock-heroic “plagued by vague doubts,” preserving the theatrical high register used for a petty suspicion. However, the second clause is mistranslated in the specific way flagged by the reference: «У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон» means each of them has LOST/has had the corresponding item involved (Shpak—tape recorder; envoy—medallion), not that they currently possess them. “Has” implies possession and weakens the detective-like inference and the callback logic. Also “envoy” is acceptable, but “ambassador” better matches the reference’s diplomatic framing; still minor compared to the possession error.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very nice, Tsar!
Very nice, hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very nice!
Hello, Tsar! Very nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation successfully preserves the comedic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness (“Very nice… hello…”) instead of archaic royal speech, matching Bunsha’s Soviet-era social clichés and drunken loopiness. It doesn’t include the self-identifying “I’m the Tsar,” but the repeated vocative “Tsar” still conveys the same absurd over-introduction. Rhythm and redundancy are maintained; overall effect is close to the reference’s intent.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Essentially identical in meaning and punch. Slightly more clipped than the reference but still forceful and in-character; no loss of comedic authoritarian flare.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the reference translation; preserves the archaic, high-register melancholy and the comic mismatch with the modern setting.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is conveyed, but the English is missing the key subject/agent relationship emphasized in the reference: “demons” are the agents who walled him in, not a vocative addressee. “Walled up, demons!” reads like he’s calling the demons “walled up,” which is semantically off. Also the ellipsis/complaint “They’ve walled me in…” better captures the Tsar’s accusatory, panicked narration; the current version is more like a label. This weakens clarity and the comedic misunderstanding of technology.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and idiomatic. It keeps the religious triumphalism and causal misattribution (cross as “cause” of the doors opening). Slightly less elevated than “Behold the power…”, but still works and reads naturally in subtitles.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The line is understandable, but the English is ungrammatical/awkward (“Everyone dance!”) and loses the showman/MC vibe that makes the moment funny (a bureaucrat suddenly acting like a host commanding a crowd). A more idiomatic imperative (“Everybody dance!” / “Everyone’s dancing!” / “All of you—dance!”) would preserve the punch. Compared to the reference’s solution, it also misses the ‘announcement’ feel.

items.translation: What a lucky time to drop by!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the upbeat self-satisfaction and opportunistic tone. “Drop by” fits the casualness; the comedic implication (he’s pleased with his criminal luck) remains. Slightly less punchy than the reference but functionally equivalent.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Imported caviar—eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The key creative warning in the reference is not to call the eggplant spread “caviar,” because English viewers will take it literally and miss the Soviet-status inversion joke. The translation keeps “Imported caviar—eggplant,” which preserves the Russian wording but risks confusion and blunts the satire (it sounds like a bizarre flavor of caviar rather than a cheap spread being grandly presented). This is a creative-accuracy issue: the joke’s mechanism is cultural/semantic, and the chosen term undermines it.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor—

gone: three tape recorders,
three film cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The list structure and escalating-count humor are partly preserved (repetition of “three”), but there is a major factual/creative distortion: the source has a single “куртка… замшевая… три” (i.e., he ends up claiming three suede jackets, but it’s delivered as a stumble: “a suede jacket… three”). The translation smooths it into “three suede jackets,” removing the comedic hesitation and, more importantly, it also changes the intermediate beat where he says “куртка… замшевая…” (singular) before landing on “три.” That stumble is part of the joke per the reference analysis. Additionally, “movie cameras” vs “film cameras” is fine, but the loss of the stumble is the key issue.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Faithful and effective: keeps the calm, rhythmic, almost sing-song resignation that makes the line funny and darkly ironic. Punctuation/line breaks work for subtitle timing; no meaningful loss.

summary.interpretation: 3.36 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.67 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
